rationale.R1.1: The section asserts a thesis (SpectralPINN for turbulent BL simulation), a gap statement (mesh-based solvers struggle with multi-scale structure), and a contribution claim (SIREN activations within a PINN framework); coherence and accuracy of these framing elements is a baseline expectation of any section review.
rationale.R1.2: Multiple logical chains require consistency checking: (1) the stated training objective is MSE to DNS ground truth, not a PDE-residual loss — this conflicts with the 'physics-informed' label and the claim about 'embedding physical priors'; (2) the ablation conclusion that activation choice is the 'principal driver' is presented on the basis of a single ablation swap, making the causal inference potentially overstated.
rationale.R1.3: Specific quantitative claims (4.2% / 6.7% L2 errors, 40× inference speedup, 9.1% tanh-ablation velocity error) are asserted inline with no reference to figures, tables, or an appendix; whether these numbers are traceable to reported evidence is a central review obligation.
rationale.R1.6: The bare delegation 'Review this section' routinely encompasses prose quality; candidate issues include the vague 'spectral approach' label (the method is a PINN with sinusoidal activations, not a spectral method), the sweeping generalization in the final paragraph, and several hedging lapses ('we expect similar gains').

parsed.clarification_question: Should I evaluate this section primarily for scientific and technical correctness (claim validity, evidence alignment, logical consistency of the physics-informed framing), or primarily for writing quality and clarity (prose, terminology precision, narrative tone)?
